es.text: No hay mal que por bien no venga
es.literal: there is no bad that does not come for good
es.meaning: Every misfortune brings something good with it.
es.example: Perdí el vuelo, pero ahí conocí a mi socia: no hay mal que por bien no venga.
es.exampleTranslation: I missed my flight, but that's where I met my business partner — every cloud has a silver lining.

sources.headword: no hay mal que por bien no venga

it.text: Non tutti i mali vengono per nuocere
it.literal: not all evils come in order to harm
it.meaning: Some misfortunes turn out to be for the best.
it.example: Ho perso il treno e ho evitato lo sciopero: non tutti i mali vengono per nuocere.
it.exampleTranslation: I missed the train and dodged the strike — some bad turns work out.

bridge.note: The same consolation offered at two different strengths. Spanish makes a universal promise — there is NO misfortune that fails to bring some good, no exceptions, said to someone who has just lost something. Italian only concedes a share: not ALL misfortunes are there to harm you, which quietly admits that some are. One language guarantees the silver lining; the other allows it. Both are built on a negation, and neither survives being translated word for word.

interference.es_to_it: The calque 'non c'è male che non venga per bene' is not the Italian proverb, and it overpromises — Italian claims some, not all.
interference.it_to_es: 'No todos los males vienen para dañar' is not a refrán; Spanish states it absolutely, with the double negative doing the work.

review.notes: Prompted by an inspirational graphic Joe shared 2026-07-18 ('el agua no sonaría tan hermosa sin piedras en su camino' — a modern composed line, not a refrán, so not bankable itself). Both proverbs and the universal-vs-partial reading are Claude's; the scope difference is the payload and needs both reviewers to confirm it is real rather than an artifact of how each is usually quoted.
